title: Treatyse of Fysshynge wyth an Angle
summary: The checked-in XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan item CME00132 and encodes the title page plus the complete treatise on printed pp. 1–37 of Thomas Satchell's 1883 An Older Form of the Treatyse of Fysshynge wyth an Angle. It excludes Satchell's presentation leaf, preface, glossary, and advertisements. Satchell printed the surviving portions of the fifteenth-century paper manuscript now Yale, Beinecke MS 171, while marking two extensive supplies from the 1496 Wynkyn de Worde printed Treatyse: from p. 9 into p. 15 and from p. 23 through the p. 37 explicit. The current Beinecke binding also interleaves Joseph Haslewood's nineteenth-century paginary transcript (pages labeled 1a–20a) with the medieval original (1b–20b); Satchell describes the transcript as a convenience but says his edition was printed from the manuscript, so the edition is related directly to the medieval pages rather than routed through Haslewood. Complete public digital objects are cached for the 1883 edition, Beinecke MS 171, and Library of Congress Rosenwald 574, whose leaves [37b]–[48b] preserve the 1496 printed treatise used here as edition-level evidence rather than as Satchell's demonstrable physical exemplar.
review_notes: The intellectual work cataloged in the parallel metadata manifest is the anonymous Middle English angling treatise, not Satchell's editorial paratext, a physical witness, the 1496 carrier volume, the digital encodings, or the facsimiles.

CME00132 includes the 1883 title page (PB REF 1) and treatise pp. 1–37 (PB REF 2–38, followed by terminal REF 39); it excludes Satchell's presentation leaf, preface i–vii, ten-page glossary, and advertisements.
The XML title page reads 'Alfred Dension'; the inspected 1883 title page and manuscript provenance establish the owner's surname as Alfred Denison.

Satchell's preface, printed preface p. iv / cached PDF p. 14, says that 'four of the earlier pages are also wanting,' whereas Yale's current catalog says three leaves are missing between manuscript pages 10 and 11. Page and leaf units are not normalized into a single physical-loss count; the discrepancy remains open pending a codicological explanation.

Satchell's first printed supply begins on p. 9 after 'the grener hyt wyl be' and ends on p. 15 before 'Wyth how many herys ye schall angle ...'; his second begins on p. 23 after the October trout-bait sentence and continues through the p. 37 explicit. The second supply has no visible terminal square bracket, so its endpoint is recorded by phrase and explicit, not by an invented mark.

The Beinecke PDF has 75 PDF pages because it adds a generated opening page to a 74-canvas IIIF sequence. The medieval original occupies the twenty canvases labeled 1b–20b; the interleaved 1a–20a pages are Haslewood's later transcript.

The Library of Congress target run is visually confirmed at PDF/images 85–107 inclusive: image 85 begins the Treatyse and image 107 contains its explicit before the next work. This corresponds to carrier leaves [37b]–[48b].

Within that full 1496 run, Satchell's first printed supply maps exactly to LOC images 90–93 / source IDs 0090–0093, from 'A nother wyse ye maye make more bryghter grene' mid-image 90 through 'now I woll tell you' near the foot of image 93. His second maps to images 99–107 / IDs 0099–0107, from 'From Aprill till Septembre the trought lepyth' mid-image 99 through the explicit on image 107.

The cached Library of Congress PDF is complete and readable as 167 pages, but qpdf reports a damaged/cross-reference reconstruction, extraneous whitespace before xref, and linearization hint-table/page-length mismatches. It is therefore not described as qpdf-clean.

Juliana Berners is a traditional and disputed attribution, not a confirmed author. The named 'Dam Julyans Barnes' explicit belongs to the hunting section of the earlier Boke tradition, and later cataloging extended that attribution to the angling treatise.
Private Git-ignored caches preserve complete provider objects; caching does not itself authorize redistribution.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00132
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's publication workflow and supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID all identify CME00132; the file is 57,514 bytes.
The only revision entry is dated 2016-08-31 and records a MURP processing step; it does not document the original encoding date or personnel.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00132
entities.description: A diplomatic-oriented electronic text of Satchell's source-edition title page and treatise pp. 1–37. It retains printed page milestones, 17 principal treatise divisions, six nested month divisions, italic expansion signals, thorn and yogh, and four Latin verse lines embedded in the prose.
notes: The stable name URL currently redirects into U-M's web platform and presented an automated Cloudflare challenge when checked; this affects automated inspection, not the identifier mapping established by the XML.

entities.label: Treatyse of Fysshynge wyth an Angle
alternate_labels: Treatise of Fishing with an Angle
An Older Form of the Treatyse of Fysshynge wyth an Angle
entities.description: The anonymous fifteenth-century Middle English prose treatise on angling: its equipment, lines, hooks, floats, methods, conditions, baits, fish, and ethical use of the sport. A traditional attribution to Juliana Berners is disputed and is not treated as established authorship.

date_statements.label: Composition or textual formation
date_statements.value: Fifteenth century, by the 1496 printed edition
date_statements.notes: This broad work-level range is distinct from Satchell and Skeat's historical suggestion that the surviving manuscript hand is earlier than 1450.

notes: This abstract work entity separates intellectual content from the incomplete manuscript, 1496 printed version, 1883 composite edition, and modern representations.

entities.label: Thomas Satchell, An Older Form of the Treatyse of Fysshynge wyth an Angle (1883)
entities.description: Satchell's edition prints the surviving medieval manuscript text and supplies missing matter from the 1496 printed Treatyse in square brackets. Its preface describes the manuscript, transmission, editorial method, and W. W. Skeat's assistance; the volume also includes a glossary and advertisements not encoded in CME00132.

bibliographic.citation: An Older Form of the Treatyse of Fysshynge wyth an Angle, attributed to Dame Juliana Barnes, printed from a MS. in the possession of Alfred Denison, Esq., with preface and glossary by Thomas Satchell (London: W. Satchell & Co., 1883).
bibliographic.title: An Older Form of the Treatyse of Fysshynge wyth an Angle
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Publications no. 41 / no. 9 in volume XIX (society not responsible for the publication)

notes: Printed by S. Peyton, 11 Bartholomew Close, E.C.
CME00132 excludes the presentation leaf, preface, glossary, and advertisements.

entities.label: Yale, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, MS 171
alternate_labels: The Treatise of Fishing with an Angle
entities.description: A fifteenth-century English paper manuscript preserving twenty pages of the treatise in one bold English secretary hand. Three leaves are missing between manuscript pages 10 and 11 and seven leaves at the end. In its present nineteenth-century format, each original page labeled 1b–20b is interleaved with Haslewood's paginary transcript labeled 1a–20a, together with added illuminations and prefatory matter.

date_statements.label: Medieval manuscript production
date_statements.notes: The current Yale catalog retains a broad fifteenth-century date; Skeat's pre-1450 view is historical opinion, not normalized as the current catalog date.

holding.notes: Purchased through A. S. W. Rosenbach after the Denison sale, later owned by David Wagstaff, and given to Yale in 1946 by Mrs. David Wagstaff.

physical_description.extent: 10 medieval paper leaves (20 original pages), interleaved with 10 modern transcript leaves (20 paginated transcript pages), plus 2 paper leaves
physical_description.format: Codex fragment; no ruling or pricking; one bold English secretary hand; original leaves glued to modern stubs
notes: Simple flourishes and initial strokes are red; leaves are stained and severely trimmed with loss of marginalia.
Three medieval leaves are missing between pages 10 and 11 and seven leaves at the end.
The nineteenth-century brick-red calf binding was gold-tooled by C. Lewis in 1823 and later rebacked.
Provenance recorded by Yale: William Herbert; Rev. John Brand; George Isted; Joseph Haslewood; Edward Jesse; Alfred Denison; A. S. W. Rosenbach; David Wagstaff; Yale.
Satchell's preface reports the 1868 Jesse-sale purchase by Denison for 45 shillings and describes five paper sheets folded quarto.

Satchell says four earlier 'pages' are wanting, while Yale says three 'leaves' are missing between manuscript pages 10 and 11. The difference in units and physical count is preserved rather than silently reconciled.

entities.label: Joseph Haslewood's paginary transcript interleaved in Beinecke MS 171
entities.description: A nineteenth-century handwritten transcript of the twenty surviving medieval pages, created for convenience and interleaved with the originals in the current bound object. Digital labels 1a–20a identify the transcript pages, while 1b–20b identify the medieval originals. The digitized order alternates by openings: it begins 1a, 1b, 2b, 2a, 3a, 3b, 4b, 4a and continues in that pattern.

holding.notes: Material component of the same present bound object as the medieval original.

physical_description.format: Paginary manuscript transcript interleaved with the medieval leaves
notes: Haslewood also added three illuminations and prefatory matter before arranging the current format and 1823 binding.
Satchell calls this a transcript made for convenience but states that his edition was printed from the manuscript; no relation routes Satchell's transcription through Haslewood.

entities.label: Yale complete digital object for Beinecke MS 171
entities.description: A 74-canvas IIIF Presentation 3 sequence from front cover through back cover, also supplied as a 75-page generated PDF. The medieval original pages are the twenty canvases labeled 1b–20b; intervening 1a–20a canvases reproduce Haslewood's transcript.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation 3 manifest with 74 canvases and a provider-generated 75-page PDF

entities.label: Wynkyn de Worde's 1496 Boke of Hawking, Hunting, and Heraldry, including the Treatyse of Fysshynge
entities.description: The Westminster 1496 printed edition commonly cataloged as the Book of Hawking, Hunting, and Heraldry. It adds the angling treatise on leaves [37b]–[48b]. Satchell says he supplied gaps from the printed Treatyse, but his precise physical copy or intervening facsimile is not identified.

bibliographic.citation: Book of hawking, hunting, and heraldry (Westminster: Wynkyn de Worde, 1496), containing 'Here begynneth the treatyse of fysshynge wyth an angle' on leaves [37b]–[48b].
bibliographic.title: Book of hawking, hunting, and heraldry

notes: The entity type reflects the lineage schema's printed-edition category; this is a historical print edition, not a modern critical edition.
The 1486 Boke did not yet contain the fishing treatise; the angling component first appears in this enlarged 1496 print.

entities.label: Library of Congress, Lessing J. Rosenwald Collection 574
entities.description: The Library of Congress copy of the 1496 Wynkyn de Worde edition, call number Incun. 1496 .B4 SK25. Its 23-surface Treatyse run is complete at digital images 85–107; the copy's cataloged facsimile substitutions at leaves [25], [67], and [74] fall outside that run.

holding.notes: Rare Book and Special Collections Division.

physical_description.format: Folio incunable with woodcuts, coats of arms, initials, and printer's device
notes: The copy is imperfect: leaves [25], [67], and [74] are supplied in facsimile; many leaves are repaired and some coats of arms are hand-colored.
None of the cataloged facsimile substitutions falls within the Treatyse on leaves [37b]–[48b].
This cataloged copy gives complete public access to the 1496 edition's target section but is not asserted to be Satchell's physical exemplar.

entities.label: Library of Congress complete digital facsimile of Rosenwald 574
entities.description: A complete 167-image/PDF digital object. Images 85–107 reproduce the Treatyse from its woodcut and incipit through its explicit; the next work begins on the facing portion of the last image.
notes: The provider PDF is readable but has qpdf structural warnings concerning xref reconstruction and linearization hint data.

entities.label: Smithsonian Libraries copy of Satchell's 1883 Older Form
entities.description: The physical Smithsonian Libraries copy digitized for the Biodiversity Heritage Library and Internet Archive, identifier 39088000805663.

entities.label: Smithsonian/BHL/Internet Archive complete scan of Satchell's 1883 Older Form
entities.description: A complete 90-page PDF of the 1883 carrier with OCR and item metadata. The CME selection is the source title page plus printed treatise pp. 1–37; the cached object also retains all excluded paratext and advertisements.
entities.reproduction_format: Complete 90-page PDF with OCR and Internet Archive metadata

agents.name: Thomas Satchell
notes: Editor of the 1883 edition. Some modern digital catalog records conflate him with his son or attach incompatible dates, so no dates are asserted here.

agents.name: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University

scope.notes: The repository file carries U-M's CME identifier family and encoded text; no intermediate copy history is documented.

subject.description: The title-page division and complete treatise division.

object.description: Source-edition title page plus the complete printed treatise, excluding other carrier matter.

scope.notes: Excluded: presentation leaf, preface i–vii, glossary [10] pages, and advertisements.

subject.description: The two surviving-manuscript runs in the printed treatise.
locators: printed p. 1 through p. 9, ending 'the grener hyt wyl be'
printed p. 15, heading 'Wyth how many herys ye schall angle ...', through p. 23, ending the October trout-bait sentence

object.description: The twenty surviving medieval original pages, not Haslewood's interleaved transcript.

scope.notes: Satchell says the edition was printed from the manuscript and brackets material supplied from the 1496 print. Yale's catalog documents missing leaves between original pages 10/11 and at the end.

subject.description: Two passages Satchell identifies by square-bracket supply from the printed Treatyse.
locators: printed p. 9, beginning '[¶ A nother wyse ye maye make more bryghter grene', through p. 15, ending 'now I woll tell you]'
printed p. 23, beginning '[From Aprill tyll Septembre þe trough lepyth', through the p. 37 explicit ending 'this meane vtterly dystroye it'

object.description: The two exact corresponding passage runs in the 1496 Treatyse.
locators: first supply: LOC PDF/images 90–93 / source IDs 0090–0093; begins mid-image 90 at 'A nother wyse ye maye make more bryghter grene' and ends near the foot of image 93 after 'now I woll tell you', before 'wyth how many heres ye shall ...'

second supply: LOC PDF/images 99–107 / source IDs 0099–0107; begins mid-image 99 after the October sentence at 'From Aprill till Septembre the trought lepyth' and continues through the explicit 'this meane vtterly dystroye it' on image 107

scope.notes: The 1496 edition-level source is confirmed, but Satchell's physical exemplar or any intervening facsimile is unidentified. The second supplied run lacks a visible terminal square bracket and is bounded by its explicit.

assertion.notes: Confirmed at textual-edition level, not at physical-copy level.

object.description: The corresponding surviving medieval original pages.

scope.notes: The current digital sequence alternates transcript and original pages by openings: 1a, 1b, 2b, 2a, 3a, 3b, 4b, 4a, and so on. Same-numbered a and b pages therefore have no uniform preceding/following order.

subject.description: All 74 provider canvases and the corresponding complete generated PDF.

object.description: The complete present bound object, including medieval leaves, Haslewood transcript, additions, binding, and covers.

scope.notes: The witness lacks three internal leaves and seven terminal leaves; the abstract work is not equated with the damaged carrier.

object.description: The abstract Middle English angling treatise.

subject.description: The edited Middle English treatise on pp. 1–37, excluding title page, preface, glossary, and advertisements.

object.description: The abstract Middle English angling treatise.

scope.notes: Satchell constructs a continuous text by combining the incomplete manuscript version with supplied passages from the 1496 print.

scope.notes: The copy's facsimile-supplied leaves [25], [67], and [74] do not affect the target leaves [37b]–[48b].

rights.component: Modern XML markup and repository-specific contribution
rights.basis: The pinned CME repository contains an Unlicense text dedicating 'software' to the public domain, but it does not expressly define whether imported textual encodings and any third-party contributions are included in that word. The medieval text and 1883 source edition are separately public-domain layers.
notes: The license text is reported exactly with its scope ambiguity; no broader ownership inference is made.

rights.component: Electronic text as distributed through the University of Michigan CME collection
rights.basis: The U-M CME collection applies a No Copyright–United States statement to its materials. This provider assertion is distinct from the local repository license and from image-provider rights.

rights.component: Medieval Middle English and embedded Latin linguistic and literary content
rights.basis: The fifteenth-century work and witnesses are centuries outside copyright term. Modern editions, markup, photographs, and provider terms remain separately scoped.

rights.component: 1883 transcription, editorial preface, glossary, arrangement, and typography
rights.basis: The edition was published in 1883 and is outside the maximum United States copyright term; the Smithsonian item also records No Copyright–United States.

rights.component: Smithsonian/BHL digitization and distributed scan files
rights.jurisdiction: United States and provider-stated CC0 dedication
rights.basis: The Smithsonian digital-library item displays CC0 and No Copyright–United States for this digitization.
The medieval text and manuscript are centuries outside copyright term. Physical ownership and rights in modern digital images remain separate.

rights.component: Joseph Haslewood's nineteenth-century transcript and arrangement
rights.basis: The transcript was created by 1823 and Haslewood died in 1833; it is outside United States copyright term. Yale's modern images are separately scoped.

rights.component: Yale manuscript photographs, generated PDF, and IIIF image delivery
rights.jurisdiction: Unknown / applicable copyright and contract jurisdictions
rights.basis: The public IIIF Presentation 3 manifest provides no rights or license field. Public viewing and download are verified, but no open reuse license is inferred.
notes: Confirm current Yale/Beinecke terms before redistributing image files, especially in commercial or print-on-demand contexts.

rights.component: 1496 printed textual content, arrangement, and typography
rights.basis: The 1496 printed edition is centuries outside copyright term. Rights and provider terms for modern reproductions are separate.

rights.component: Library of Congress digital images, PDF, and access layer for Rosenwald 574
rights.jurisdiction: United States; other jurisdictions may differ
rights.basis: The Library of Congress states that it is not aware of U.S. copyright or other restrictions in the collection, while warning that other nations' law, privacy, publicity, and third-party rights may apply and that users must make their own assessment.
notes: Credit line: Library of Congress, Rare Book and Special Collections Division.
This is recorded as a provider claim, not as a universal public-domain declaration.

editorial_practices.description: Satchell expands manuscript abbreviations and marks supplied expansion letters typographically in italics; CME00132 retains those italic spans.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Manuscript orthography, scribal errors, punctuation, thorn, yogh, and possible final-e flourishes
editorial_practices.representation: Orthography, errors, and punctuation generally preserved; thorn and yogh retained; doubtful or damaged letters bracketed; possible final-e flourish usually not printed
editorial_practices.description: The practice category records Satchell's explicit treatment of punctuation and related diplomatic features; it is chiefly preservation with a stated exception for uncertain final-e flourishes, not silent modernization.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Missing internal and terminal leaves in the medieval witness
editorial_practices.representation: Corresponding passages selected from the 1496 printed Treatyse and marked by square brackets
editorial_practices.description: Satchell makes a continuous edition by supplying two gaps from the 1496 print. The first supply is visibly closed on p. 15; the terminal supply continues from p. 23 through the explicit without a visible closing bracket.

editorial_practices.representation: Only the source-edition title page and treatise pp. 1–37 are encoded
editorial_practices.description: The U-M item selects the title page and complete edited treatise while omitting the presentation leaf, preface, glossary, and advertisements.
editorial_practices.scope: PB REF 1–39; both XML DIV1 elements

It also locates Satchell's first supplied passage exactly on images 90–93 / source IDs 0090–0093 and his second on images 99–107 / IDs 0099–0107, with both phrase boundaries visible on the endpoint surfaces.

evidence.citation: Julia Boffey, 'Berners [Bernes, Barnes], Juliana (fl. 1460), supposed author and prioress of Sopwell,' Oxford Dictionary of National Biography.
evidence.summary: Authoritative modern biographical scholarship frames Berners as a supposed author rather than a securely identified author, supporting explicit treatment of the attribution as disputed.

evidence.citation: Women's Literary Culture and the Medieval Canon, University of Surrey, 'Juliana Berners (fl. 1460), writer,' 24 February 2025.
evidence.locator: Discussion of the 1486 hunting explicit, 1496 fishing addition, later extension of attribution, and modern dispute
evidence.summary: Explains that the named Juliana explicit belongs to the hunting section, that the fishing treatise was added in the 1496 reprint, and that later attribution of the entire compilation—including fishing—to Berners is questioned by scholars.

open_questions.question: Can any evidence securely attribute the angling treatise itself to Juliana Berners, rather than extending the hunting section's named explicit to the later 1496 fishing addition?
next_steps: Consult Julia Boffey's full ODNB discussion and the specialized studies cited by the Yale catalog, especially John McDonald and W. L. Braekman.
Keep the 1486 hunting explicit, the anonymous 1496 fishing text, and later compilation-level catalog attributions separate.
Retain anonymous authorship with a disputed traditional attribution unless document-level evidence identifies the angling author.
notes: Provider creator headings and Satchell's title-page phrase 'attributed to' are evidence of attribution history, not proof of authorship.

open_questions.question: Which physical copy, facsimile, or intermediary of the 1496 printed Treatyse did Satchell use for his two supplied passages?
next_steps: Inspect Satchell's correspondence in the Beinecke files and contemporary publication records for an identified print or facsimile.
Compare Satchell's supplied readings against multiple 1496 copies and nineteenth-century facsimiles for copy-specific or intermediary variants.
Do not identify Library of Congress Rosenwald 574 as Satchell's exemplar without provenance or textual evidence.

open_questions.question: How should Satchell's statement that four earlier manuscript pages were wanting be reconciled with Yale's current statement that three leaves are missing between manuscript pages 10 and 11?
next_steps: Ask Beinecke staff for the quire/stub evidence supporting the current three-leaf loss statement.
Inspect Satchell's correspondence and any older collations to determine what he counted as a 'page.'
Do not convert pages to leaves or choose a single internal-loss count without a physical-structure crosswalk.
notes: Satchell's statement is at printed preface p. iv / cached PDF p. 14; Yale locates its three missing leaves between manuscript pages 10 and 11.

open_questions.question: When and where was the angling treatise composed, as distinct from the date and dialect of Beinecke MS 171 and the 1496 Westminster printing?
next_steps: Consult modern linguistic and textual studies rather than normalizing Skeat's pre-1450 opinion as a settled work date.
Compare manuscript and 1496 versions to distinguish textual formation from physical witness production.
Keep England as cultural origin while leaving narrower composition place unknown unless independent evidence supports it.

open_questions.question: What current reuse terms govern Yale's MS 171 digital photographs and generated PDF?
next_steps: Check Yale and Beinecke reproduction policies at the point of intended use.
Ask Yale for a work-specific rights determination if images are to be redistributed commercially or in print-on-demand output.
Do not infer an open license solely from public IIIF and PDF access.
notes: The IIIF manifest's rights field is absent/null; its requiredStatement only names Yale University Library as provider.
